concept: Azure AI Foundry project connection using managed identity for Azure AI Search
scenario: An Azure AI engineer is configuring a Foundry project for an internal claims assistant that must query Azure AI Search without storing search API keys in application settings.
decisive_constraint: Security approval requires Microsoft Entra authentication and least-privilege role assignment on the search service.
target_misconception: Candidates confuse a governed Foundry connection with copying admin keys or user-scoped secrets into the application runtime.
correct_answer: Create a Foundry project connection that uses managed identity or keyless credentials, then grant that identity only the required Azure AI Search role.
distractor_1: Store the Azure AI Search admin key in Key Vault and load it at startup - wrong because the application still authenticates with a broad shared key rather than the approved identity.
distractor_2: Generate a storage SAS token for the index data source - wrong because SAS tokens authorize storage access, not query calls to Azure AI Search from the assistant.
distractor_3: Issue a separate search API key to each signed-in user - wrong because Azure AI Search API keys are service credentials and do not enforce per-user Entra role assignments.
not_a_variant_of: Existing concepts cover managed workspaces and RAG grounding; this row focuses on keyless Foundry project connections and identity-based access to Azure AI Search.

concept: Azure OpenAI model deployment capacity selection for predictable high-volume traffic
scenario: A production chat application on Azure OpenAI has a steady 800 requests per minute workload and must keep latency predictable during a quarterly enrollment window.
decisive_constraint: The team needs reserved model throughput instead of best-effort consumption that can be throttled by shared quota.
target_misconception: Candidates assume scaling the web app tier solves model-side capacity limits or choose a cheaper deployment mode despite predictable high load.
correct_answer: Deploy the required model with provisioned throughput capacity sized for the expected request and token volume.
distractor_1: Use a standard pay-as-you-go deployment and retry every throttled request - wrong because retries do not reserve capacity and can worsen latency during the traffic spike.
distractor_2: Increase the App Service instance count behind the chat API - wrong because more web workers do not increase Azure OpenAI model throughput quota.
distractor_3: Switch to an embedding model deployment for the chat turns - wrong because embeddings produce vectors for retrieval and do not generate conversational responses.
not_a_variant_of: Existing concepts cover low-cost model selection and small-versus-large model tradeoffs; this row tests reserved deployment capacity for predictable production load.

concept: Azure AI Foundry agent required-action loop for custom function tool outputs
scenario: A Foundry agent run calls a custom refund-status function and the orchestration service receives a required action instead of a final assistant message.
decisive_constraint: The conversation must continue in the same thread after the backend lookup completes so the agent can use the returned status.
target_misconception: Candidates expect the model to execute external code by itself or lose conversation state by starting a new thread after tool execution.
correct_answer: Execute the requested function in the application, submit the tool output back to the same run or thread, and let the agent continue.
distractor_1: Ask the model to call the refund API URL directly from its next response - wrong because the model emits tool requests and the application executes the backend call.
distractor_2: Start a new agent thread containing only the refund status - wrong because the prior run state and tool-call requirement are not satisfied.
distractor_3: Append the lookup result as an assistant message without submitting tool outputs - wrong because the run remains blocked on the required action.
not_a_variant_of: Existing concepts cover tool schemas and approved business functions; this row tests the runtime handoff for required tool actions and submitted outputs.

concept: Azure AI Foundry tracing and token analytics for agent latency diagnosis
scenario: A Foundry agent serving travel-policy questions shows p95 latency increasing from 4 seconds to 13 seconds after adding two custom tools and three retrieved passages per turn.
decisive_constraint: The engineers must separate model latency, retrieval and tool-call latency, and token growth before changing the architecture.
target_misconception: Candidates jump to a model upgrade or remove safeguards without first using run traces and token metrics to isolate the latency source.
correct_answer: Inspect Foundry tracing spans for the model call, retrieval step, and each custom tool invocation.
correct_answer_2: Review token usage and latency metrics for the affected agent runs before reducing prompts or retrieved context.
distractor_1: Switch immediately to the largest available model - wrong because a larger model may increase latency and does not identify whether tools, retrieval, or tokens caused the regression.
distractor_2: Disable safety filtering for all travel-policy responses - wrong because safety checks are not proven to be the latency bottleneck and removing them weakens the required guardrails.
not_a_variant_of: Existing concepts mention token budgeting and context overflow; this row focuses on trace-based latency decomposition across model, retrieval, and tool spans.

concept: Azure AI Computer Vision image analysis for captions and object regions
scenario: A support portal lets customers upload appliance photos and must identify visible damaged parts while also generating concise alt text for accessibility.
decisive_constraint: The input is a natural image and the output needs captions plus object or region-level visual analysis.
target_misconception: Candidates confuse document OCR and text extraction services with image-analysis features for photos.
correct_answer: Use Azure AI Computer Vision image analysis capabilities for captions, tags, and object or region detection.
distractor_1: Use Azure AI Document Intelligence prebuilt invoice extraction - wrong because it targets structured documents and fields rather than visual objects in appliance photos.
distractor_2: Use Azure AI Speech transcription - wrong because the input is an image, not an audio stream.
distractor_3: Use Azure AI Search hybrid retrieval directly on the uploaded photo - wrong because search retrieval requires indexed content or vectors and does not itself analyze the image regions.
not_a_variant_of: Existing concepts cover PDF text extraction and RAG indexing; this row tests natural-image captioning and object-region analysis.

concept: Azure OpenAI image editing with mask-based inpainting
scenario: A marketing workflow must replace the background of a product photo while keeping the product pixels unchanged for catalog consistency.
decisive_constraint: Only the masked background region may be regenerated and the original product image must remain the reference.
target_misconception: Candidates treat image editing as a text-only generation prompt or use OCR-oriented services that cannot preserve unmasked regions.
correct_answer: Use an image-editing or inpainting request with the original image and a mask that selects only the background.
distractor_1: Send a text-only prompt asking for a similar product on a new background - wrong because it can regenerate the product itself instead of preserving the original pixels.
distractor_2: Run Document Intelligence layout analysis on the photo - wrong because layout analysis extracts document structure and does not edit image regions.
distractor_3: Create a hybrid search index over prior catalog images - wrong because retrieval can find similar assets but does not modify the uploaded image.
not_a_variant_of: Existing concepts cover embeddings, chunking, and PDF extraction; this row tests masked image editing for a visual generation workflow.

concept: Azure AI Speech real-time translation with custom vocabulary for agent input
scenario: A voice-enabled support agent must convert live Spanish calls into English text before summarization and must recognize the company's product acronyms accurately.
decisive_constraint: The pipeline needs near-real-time audio translation plus domain vocabulary adaptation before the LLM receives text.
target_misconception: Candidates send raw audio to a text-only language model or use document services that cannot perform real-time speech recognition.
correct_answer: Use Azure AI Speech speech translation with custom speech or phrase configuration for the product acronyms.
distractor_1: Use Azure OpenAI text completions directly on the audio stream - wrong because a text completion endpoint does not perform specialized real-time speech recognition and translation.
distractor_2: Use Azure AI Document Intelligence layout extraction - wrong because it processes document files, not live spoken audio.
distractor_3: Use Azure AI Computer Vision OCR on call recordings - wrong because OCR extracts text from images and cannot transcribe or translate speech.
not_a_variant_of: Existing concepts focus on prompts, memory, and retrieval; this row introduces speech translation and custom vocabulary for voice agents.

concept: Schema-constrained generative extraction from unstructured support emails
scenario: A claims intake service receives unstructured support emails and must extract policy number, incident date, sentiment, and safety issue flags into a downstream JSON contract.
decisive_constraint: Downstream processing rejects undeclared fields and cannot accept fabricated values when the email omits a required fact.
target_misconception: Candidates rely on a general summarization prompt or a single-purpose sentiment API when the workflow requires structured extraction with validation.
correct_answer: Use a generative extraction prompt or structured-output configuration that constrains the response to the required JSON schema.
correct_answer_2: Validate the generated JSON against the contract and require null or an explicit absent value when source evidence is missing.
distractor_1: Run OCR over the email body before extraction - wrong because the email is already text and OCR does not enforce the JSON contract.
distractor_2: Call only a sentiment analysis endpoint - wrong because sentiment alone does not extract policy numbers, incident dates, or safety flags into the required structure.
not_a_variant_of: Existing concepts cover a generic JSON response requirement in a system prompt; this row tests evidence-bound structured extraction from unstructured text.

concept: Azure AI Document Intelligence layout and field extraction for scanned invoices
scenario: An accounts-payable agent receives scanned invoices with tables and key-value pairs and must pass vendor, invoice total, line items, and page layout into a RAG workflow.
decisive_constraint: The extraction must preserve document structure rather than returning only plain OCR text.
target_misconception: Candidates assume basic OCR or search indexing is enough for structured invoice fields and table layout.
correct_answer: Use Azure AI Document Intelligence document analysis for layout, tables, key-value pairs, and invoice fields before indexing the extracted content.
distractor_1: Use basic image OCR and concatenate all detected text - wrong because plain OCR loses table and key-value structure needed by the downstream workflow.
distractor_2: Create an Azure AI Search indexer over the image files without enrichment - wrong because search indexing alone does not extract invoice fields from scanned pages.
distractor_3: Use Azure AI Speech batch transcription - wrong because the source is scanned invoice images, not spoken audio.
not_a_variant_of: Existing concepts include PDF text extraction; this row tests structured layout and field extraction for invoice documents.

concept: Content Understanding analyzer output for multimodal meeting evidence
scenario: A compliance workflow ingests meeting recordings that include spoken discussion, shared-screen text, and slide images, then routes structured evidence to an agent for review.
decisive_constraint: The workflow must analyze multiple media types and return structured outputs that downstream agent steps can consume.
target_misconception: Candidates pick a single-modality service and miss that the source evidence spans audio, video frames, and visual text.
correct_answer: Use a Content Understanding analyzer or Foundry multimodal workflow that extracts structured information across the audio, video, and visual content.
distractor_1: Use only Azure AI Speech transcription - wrong because transcription captures spoken words but misses slide images and shared-screen visual text.
distractor_2: Use only Azure AI Computer Vision captioning on sampled frames - wrong because image captions omit the spoken discussion and do not produce the full structured evidence.
distractor_3: Use only Azure AI Search hybrid ranking over the file names - wrong because ranking file metadata does not extract multimodal content for the agent.
not_a_variant_of: Existing concepts cover text RAG, hybrid search, and PDF extraction; this row focuses on multimodal analyzer output for audio, video, and visual evidence.

concept: Azure AI Foundry network isolation with private endpoints for project resources
scenario: An Azure AI engineer is deploying an Azure AI Foundry project that must call Azure OpenAI and Azure Storage without allowing public network access to either resource.
decisive_constraint: Security policy requires traffic to remain on private Azure networking while the project still uses managed Foundry resources.
target_misconception: Candidates confuse identity authorization with network isolation, assuming RBAC or key rotation prevents public endpoint exposure.
correct_answer: Configure private networking for the Azure AI Foundry hub or project and connected Azure resources using private endpoints.
distractor_1: Assign the Cognitive Services OpenAI User role only — wrong because RBAC authorizes callers but does not remove public network reachability.
distractor_2: Rotate the Azure OpenAI API key and store it in Key Vault — wrong because key management does not force traffic over a private endpoint.
distractor_3: Lower the content-filter severity threshold on the model deployment — wrong because content filters inspect prompts and responses, not network paths.
not_a_variant_of: No prior question in this exam or existing_concepts covers Azure AI Foundry private endpoint network isolation.

concept: Azure AI Foundry Agent Code Interpreter tool for conversational Python analysis
scenario: A support analyst uploads a CSV of failed transactions to an Azure AI Foundry agent and asks the agent to calculate grouped totals and generate a chart during the same conversation.
decisive_constraint: The agent must execute temporary Python analysis against the uploaded file without calling a customer backend API.
target_misconception: Candidates confuse Code Interpreter, which runs sandboxed analysis over files, with function tools or file search that only call external operations or retrieve text.
correct_answer: Enable the Code Interpreter tool for the agent and attach the CSV file to the conversation or tool resource.
distractor_1: Define a custom function tool for calculate_totals — wrong because the scenario needs ad hoc Python execution on an uploaded file, not a fixed backend API call.
distractor_2: Add File Search over the CSV — wrong because file search retrieves relevant content but does not execute Python calculations or produce charts.
distractor_3: Create an Azure AI Search semantic index for the CSV — wrong because semantic ranking is retrieval-oriented and does not run tabular analysis code.
not_a_variant_of: Prior planned concepts include custom function output handling but do not cover Code Interpreter file analysis.

concept: Azure AI Foundry Agent File Search grounding with indexed private policy documents
scenario: An Azure AI Foundry agent must answer employee benefit questions from a curated set of HR policy PDFs that change monthly.
decisive_constraint: Both retrieval grounding and scoped document availability must be configured so the model does not rely on stale model knowledge.
target_misconception: Candidates know that RAG is needed but confuse agent File Search setup with fine-tuning or placing whole documents in the system prompt.
correct_answer: Add a File Search tool to the agent so it can retrieve relevant passages from the indexed HR documents.
correct_answer_2: Attach or upload the policy files to the file search vector store or tool resources used by that agent.
distractor_1: Fine-tune the base model on the policy PDFs — wrong because monthly policy changes are better handled by updating searchable grounding data than retraining model weights.
distractor_2: Paste the full policy corpus into the system prompt — wrong because large changing documents exceed practical context limits and are not a managed retrieval source.
not_a_variant_of: Existing_concepts covers generic RAG and Azure AI Search patterns; this row specifically covers the Azure AI Foundry Agent File Search tool resources.

concept: Azure AI Content Safety Prompt Shields for indirect prompt injection in grounded content
scenario: A grounded Azure AI assistant starts following malicious instructions hidden inside retrieved web pages, even though the user prompt itself is benign.
decisive_constraint: The risk comes from untrusted retrieved content attempting to override the assistant's instructions before generation.
target_misconception: Candidates treat all safety screening as generic harmful-content classification and miss the specific indirect prompt injection defense.
correct_answer: Use Azure AI Content Safety Prompt Shields to detect and block indirect prompt injection attacks from retrieved content.
distractor_1: Use sentiment analysis on retrieved passages — wrong because sentiment detects positive or negative tone, not instructions that try to hijack the model.
distractor_2: Increase the model temperature — wrong because higher randomness does not detect malicious instructions and can make behavior less predictable.
distractor_3: Add more few-shot examples of safe answers — wrong because examples do not inspect untrusted retrieved content for injection attempts.
not_a_variant_of: Existing_concepts covers broad harmful prompt and response filtering, not indirect prompt injection detection in retrieved grounding data.

concept: Azure AI Language PII detection and redaction before indexing support transcripts
scenario: A team is preparing customer support transcripts for an agent knowledge base and must remove phone numbers, email addresses, and government identifiers before embedding.
decisive_constraint: The required output is redacted text plus entity categories so reviewers can confirm which sensitive fields were removed.
target_misconception: Candidates confuse language PII recognition with document layout extraction, content moderation, or translation features.
correct_answer: Run Azure AI Language personally identifiable information recognition and use its redacted text output before indexing.
distractor_1: Run Azure AI Content Safety hate and violence classification — wrong because those categories detect harmful content, not specific PII entity spans for redaction.
distractor_2: Use Azure AI Document Intelligence layout extraction only — wrong because layout extraction identifies page structure but does not classify and redact PII entities in transcript text.
distractor_3: Translate the transcripts to a neutral language first — wrong because translation does not remove sensitive identifiers and may preserve the same PII.
not_a_variant_of: No prior planned concept or existing_concepts entry covers Azure AI Language PII redaction for text preparation.

concept: Azure AI Speech SSML pronunciation and speaking style for agent text-to-speech
scenario: A voice agent reads medication names aloud and the product owner needs deterministic pronunciation plus a calmer speaking style for the synthesized response.
decisive_constraint: The fix must control speech synthesis output without retraining a speech recognition model.
target_misconception: Candidates confuse text-to-speech synthesis markup with speech-to-text customization or translation vocabulary settings.
correct_answer: Use Speech Synthesis Markup Language with pronunciation and voice style elements in the text-to-speech request.
distractor_1: Train a custom speech recognition model — wrong because recognition models improve audio-to-text transcription, not how synthesized speech pronounces output.
distractor_2: Enable real-time speech translation — wrong because translation changes language output and does not provide deterministic pronunciation controls for TTS.
distractor_3: Add Content Safety text moderation before synthesis — wrong because moderation can block unsafe text but does not control voice style or phonemes.
not_a_variant_of: Prior planned concepts cover speech translation with custom vocabulary, not SSML control for text-to-speech synthesis.

concept: Azure AI Document Intelligence custom neural model for handwritten intake form extraction
scenario: An insurance application receives thousands of scanned handwritten intake forms whose field labels and box positions differ from the prebuilt invoice and receipt formats.
decisive_constraint: The extraction must return named business fields from the organization's own form type after training on labeled examples.
target_misconception: Candidates confuse generic layout OCR or prebuilt models with a custom model trained for a specific document schema.
correct_answer: Train a custom neural Document Intelligence model using labeled samples of the intake form fields.
distractor_1: Use the prebuilt invoice model — wrong because the forms are not invoices and require organization-specific fields absent from the invoice schema.
distractor_2: Use only the layout model — wrong because layout returns text, tables, and selection marks but does not learn the target business field schema.
distractor_3: Use Azure AI Search semantic ranking directly on the scanned images — wrong because semantic ranking requires indexed text and does not extract form fields from images.
not_a_variant_of: Prior planned concepts cover layout and field extraction for scanned invoices; this row covers training a custom neural model for a different handwritten form schema.

concept: Azure AI Speech conversation transcription diarization and profanity handling for agent notes
scenario: A call-center agent assistant must transcribe live two-speaker calls, label which participant said each utterance, and mask profanity before creating case notes.
decisive_constraint: Both speaker attribution and profanity masking must be configured in the speech-to-text pipeline before the notes are summarized.
target_misconception: Candidates know speech transcription is required but confuse diarization and profanity options with translation or custom voice synthesis.
correct_answer: Use conversation transcription or diarization so the transcript identifies separate speakers.
correct_answer_2: Set the Speech service profanity handling option to mask or remove profane words in the transcription output.
distractor_1: Enable neural voice speaking styles — wrong because speaking styles affect synthesized audio output, not speaker labels or profanity in speech-to-text transcripts.
distractor_2: Configure speech translation target languages — wrong because translation changes transcript language and does not by itself assign speakers or mask profanity.
not_a_variant_of: Prior planned concepts cover speech translation and custom vocabulary for agent input; this row covers diarized transcription and profanity handling.

concept: Azure AI Foundry Agent threads for persistent multi-turn conversation state
scenario: A web app uses an Azure AI Foundry agent for case triage and must preserve the same user's prior messages and tool results across several turns in one support session.
decisive_constraint: The application needs server-side conversation state tied to the agent run instead of resending the entire chat history manually each time.
target_misconception: Candidates confuse persistent agent threads with creating new agents, relying on stateless chat completions, or storing all memory in the system prompt.
correct_answer: Create or reuse an agent thread for the session and add each user message to that thread before starting runs.
distractor_1: Create a new agent for every user message — wrong because agents define behavior and tools, while threads hold the session conversation state.
distractor_2: Call a stateless chat completion request without a stored thread — wrong because the service will not retain prior messages or tool outputs unless the app resends them.
distractor_3: Append the full conversation to the system instructions — wrong because system instructions define durable behavior and are not the managed per-session state container.
not_a_variant_of: Existing_concepts covers generic memory and context-window strategies; this row specifically covers Azure AI Foundry Agent thread state.

concept: Azure OpenAI realtime audio session for low-latency voice agents
scenario: A kiosk assistant must let users interrupt the model while speaking and receive synthesized spoken answers with minimal round-trip latency.
decisive_constraint: The interaction requires bidirectional audio streaming rather than a batch speech-to-text request followed by a separate text-to-speech request.
target_misconception: Candidates confuse low-latency realtime audio models with separate Speech service transcription and synthesis pipelines.
correct_answer: Use an Azure OpenAI realtime audio-capable model and realtime session for bidirectional speech input and output.
distractor_1: Submit each utterance to batch transcription and then call text-to-speech after the full response is complete — wrong because the sequential pipeline adds latency and does not support natural interruption.
distractor_2: Use Azure AI Search semantic answers over audio files — wrong because semantic answers retrieve indexed text and do not provide live bidirectional voice interaction.
distractor_3: Fine-tune an embedding model on sample conversations — wrong because embeddings support retrieval similarity, not realtime audio input and synthesized output.
not_a_variant_of: No prior planned concept or existing_concepts entry covers realtime Azure OpenAI audio sessions for voice agents.

concept: Azure AI Foundry least-privilege RBAC for model deployment management
scenario: An Azure AI engineer must let a release operator create and update model deployments in an Azure AI Foundry project without granting permissions to change networking or delete the hub.
decisive_constraint: The operator needs deployment lifecycle permissions only, while hub administration and infrastructure settings must remain with the platform team.
target_misconception: Candidates confuse Azure AI Foundry project-scoped RBAC roles for model deployment work with broader Azure subscription Owner or Contributor permissions.
correct_answer: Assign an Azure AI Foundry role that grants deployment management at the project or hub scope required for the operator's deployment tasks.
distractor_1: Assign Azure subscription Owner — wrong because Owner allows broad resource and access-management changes beyond model deployment lifecycle work.
distractor_2: Store the deployment API key in the operator's local profile — wrong because a local key does not provide governed Azure RBAC authorization or least-privilege access.
distractor_3: Make the operator a hub administrator — wrong because hub administration includes platform-level management that exceeds the stated deployment-only requirement.
not_a_variant_of: No prior question covers Azure AI Foundry RBAC role selection for deployment-only operational access.

concept: Azure OpenAI quota and retry handling for 429 throttling during load tests
scenario: A chat application load test against an Azure OpenAI deployment returns bursts of HTTP 429 responses when concurrent users send long prompts.
decisive_constraint: The fix must address both the deployment's token-rate capacity and transient throttling behavior without changing the model's answer quality.
target_misconception: Candidates treat 429 responses as prompt-quality failures instead of capacity and rate-limit signals that require quota planning plus resilient client behavior.
correct_answer: Review the deployment's assigned quota and increase or redistribute token-per-minute capacity for the target region and model.
correct_answer_2: Implement client retry logic with backoff for rate-limit responses instead of immediately failing the user request.
distractor_1: Lower the system prompt's safety rules — wrong because safety instructions do not increase the service quota or make rate-limit responses disappear.
distractor_2: Enable File Search on the agent — wrong because grounding files changes retrieval behavior, not the Azure OpenAI deployment's rate-limit capacity.
not_a_variant_of: q02 covers choosing deployment capacity for predictable traffic; this covers diagnosing 429 throttling with quota and retry handling.

concept: Azure AI Foundry Agent OpenAPI tool using an authenticated project connection
scenario: An Azure AI Foundry agent must call an existing inventory REST API whose operations are already described by an OpenAPI specification and require bearer-token authentication.
decisive_constraint: The agent should use the published operation schema and a governed connection rather than hand-written free-form HTTP instructions.
target_misconception: Candidates confuse OpenAPI tools, custom function tools, and retrieval tools when the external system is already exposed as a schema-described REST API.
correct_answer: Add the API as an OpenAPI tool and bind its authentication through an Azure AI Foundry project connection.
distractor_1: Use Code Interpreter to issue ad hoc HTTP requests — wrong because Code Interpreter is for sandboxed analysis code and does not provide a governed API operation schema.
distractor_2: Upload the OpenAPI file to File Search — wrong because File Search grounds answers from documents but does not turn REST operations into callable tools.
distractor_3: Describe the API endpoint only in the system prompt — wrong because prompt text cannot enforce the OpenAPI parameter schema or managed authentication.
not_a_variant_of: q03 covers custom function required-action outputs; this covers OpenAPI tools for an existing authenticated REST API.

concept: Azure AI Foundry connected agents for specialist handoff orchestration
scenario: A support solution needs a primary triage agent to hand billing cases to a billing specialist agent and technical cases to a diagnostics specialist agent while preserving the conversation goal.
decisive_constraint: Each specialist should keep its own instructions and tools, and the primary agent should coordinate handoffs instead of embedding all tools into one agent.
target_misconception: Candidates over-centralize every tool and instruction in a single agent instead of using connected agents for delegated specialist workflows.
correct_answer: Configure connected agents so the primary agent can delegate to specialist agents with their own tools and instructions.
distractor_1: Merge all billing and diagnostics tools into the primary agent — wrong because it removes specialist boundaries and makes tool selection harder to govern.
distractor_2: Use a vector store for the specialist routing rules — wrong because retrieval can provide reference content but does not execute agent-to-agent handoffs.
distractor_3: Increase the model temperature for the primary agent — wrong because sampling randomness does not create governed specialist orchestration.
not_a_variant_of: No prior question covers connected-agent handoffs or multi-agent orchestration in Azure AI Foundry.

concept: Azure AI Content Safety image harm-category classification before generated media publishing
scenario: A media workflow must block generated promotional images that contain sexual, violent, hate, or self-harm content before the images are published to a public gallery.
decisive_constraint: The moderation step must classify visual content by safety harm category rather than inspect only the user's text prompt.
target_misconception: Candidates assume text prompt filters are sufficient for generated images and overlook image-content safety classification.
correct_answer: Run the generated image through Azure AI Content Safety image moderation for harm-category classification before publishing.
distractor_1: Use sentiment analysis on the image prompt — wrong because sentiment analysis evaluates text polarity and does not inspect the generated pixels for visual harm categories.
distractor_2: Add a watermark to the image — wrong because a watermark can identify generated media but does not classify unsafe visual content.
distractor_3: Store the image in a private blob container only after publishing — wrong because storage access does not perform pre-publication safety screening.
not_a_variant_of: q14 covers Prompt Shields for indirect prompt injection; this covers image harm-category moderation for generated media.

concept: Azure AI Language sentiment analysis with opinion mining for product feedback
scenario: A customer-experience agent must summarize whether product reviews are positive or negative and identify which product aspects, such as battery life or shipping, drove each sentiment.
decisive_constraint: The pipeline needs aspect-level opinions, not only an overall generated summary of each review.
target_misconception: Candidates confuse generic LLM summarization with the Azure AI Language sentiment and opinion-mining capability that returns aspect-level sentiment.
correct_answer: Use Azure AI Language sentiment analysis with opinion mining enabled for the review text.
distractor_1: Use entity recognition alone — wrong because entity recognition can identify named entities but does not assign positive or negative sentiment to product aspects.
distractor_2: Use speech-to-text transcription — wrong because transcription converts audio to text and does not analyze opinion polarity in written reviews.
distractor_3: Use Azure AI Search semantic ranking — wrong because semantic ranking orders search results and does not produce aspect-level sentiment labels.
not_a_variant_of: No prior question covers Azure AI Language sentiment analysis or opinion mining for aspect-level feedback.

concept: Azure AI Document Intelligence prebuilt general document model for key-value extraction
scenario: An intake workflow receives varied PDF forms from many vendors and must extract paragraphs, tables, selection marks, and generic key-value pairs without training a custom model.
decisive_constraint: The document layouts vary enough that a fixed invoice model is not appropriate, but the team cannot label training data this sprint.
target_misconception: Candidates choose custom training or a specialized prebuilt model when the requirement is generic extraction from varied documents without labels.
correct_answer: Use the Document Intelligence prebuilt general document model to extract layout elements and generic key-value pairs.
distractor_1: Train a custom neural model first — wrong because custom neural extraction requires labeled examples and training, which the team does not have time to prepare.
distractor_2: Use the prebuilt invoice model — wrong because the inputs are varied vendor forms, not invoices with invoice-specific fields.
distractor_3: Use Azure AI Search vectorization directly on the PDFs — wrong because vectorization supports retrieval but does not extract structured key-value pairs from the document layout.
not_a_variant_of: q09 covers layout and field extraction for scanned invoices; q17 covers custom neural models for handwritten forms.

concept: Azure AI Foundry prompt flow deployment from version-controlled flow assets
scenario: A team has tested a prompt flow locally and must promote the same flow definition through dev and production with repeatable deployments from source-controlled assets.
decisive_constraint: The release process must deploy the tested flow artifact rather than rebuilding the flow manually in the portal for each environment.
target_misconception: Candidates treat prompt flow promotion as a manual portal task instead of packaging and deploying versioned flow assets.
correct_answer: Use the Azure AI Foundry prompt flow deployment workflow to deploy the version-controlled flow as an endpoint or managed deployment.
distractor_1: Copy the prompt text into a new playground session — wrong because a playground session is not a repeatable deployment artifact for multiple environments.
distractor_2: Export only the chat transcript from testing — wrong because transcripts show sample behavior but do not contain the flow definition and runtime configuration.
distractor_3: Increase the model quota before every promotion — wrong because quota affects capacity, not whether the same prompt flow asset is deployed consistently.
not_a_variant_of: No prior question covers prompt flow deployment or CI/CD-style promotion of versioned flow assets.

concept: Azure AI Foundry quality and safety monitoring for production generative apps
scenario: After releasing a grounded chat app, the product team wants scheduled production monitoring that tracks answer quality and safety signals over time instead of relying only on pre-release test results.
decisive_constraint: The monitoring must surface ongoing regressions in generated responses after deployment, not just measure a static evaluation dataset once.
target_misconception: Candidates confuse one-time offline evaluation with ongoing Azure AI Foundry monitoring for production quality and safety trends.
correct_answer: Configure Azure AI Foundry quality and safety monitoring for the deployed generative AI application.
distractor_1: Run a single manual prompt test in the playground each month — wrong because isolated manual tests do not provide systematic production monitoring or trend detection.
distractor_2: Increase retrieval chunk size globally — wrong because chunk size can affect grounding but does not monitor answer quality or safety after release.
distractor_3: Store full user prompts in application logs only — wrong because raw logging alone does not evaluate response quality or safety metrics.
not_a_variant_of: Existing concepts include pre-release groundedness evaluation; this row covers ongoing production quality and safety monitoring.

concept: Azure AI Document Intelligence markdown output for RAG-friendly document ingestion
scenario: A RAG ingestion pipeline processes long PDFs with headings, tables, and formulas, and retrieved chunks lose important structure when the extractor emits plain text only.
decisive_constraint: The downstream chunker needs preserved document structure and table semantics before embeddings are generated.
target_misconception: Candidates assume OCR plain text is enough for RAG ingestion and miss Document Intelligence markdown output features that preserve structure for chunking.
correct_answer: Request Document Intelligence output in markdown format so headings and structural elements are preserved for downstream chunking.
correct_answer_2: Keep table representations from the markdown output so retrieval chunks retain row and column context.
distractor_1: Strip all headings before embedding — wrong because removing headings discards structure that helps chunks remain interpretable and grounded.
distractor_2: Use only image captions for every page — wrong because captions summarize visual content but do not preserve the document's text hierarchy, tables, and formulas.
not_a_variant_of: q09 covers extracting fields from scanned invoices; this covers markdown output structure for RAG ingestion.

concept: Azure OpenAI keyless authentication with Microsoft Entra managed identity
scenario: A Python app running in Azure Container Apps must call an Azure OpenAI model deployment without storing API keys in code, container images, or app settings.
decisive_constraint: Security review requires credential rotation through Microsoft Entra ID and authorization scoped by Azure RBAC on the Azure OpenAI resource.
target_misconception: Candidates confuse keyless Microsoft Entra authentication with simply hiding an API key in a Foundry connection or environment variable.
correct_answer: Use DefaultAzureCredential with the app's managed identity and grant the identity the Cognitive Services OpenAI User role on the Azure OpenAI resource.
distractor_1: Store the Azure OpenAI API key in an app setting - wrong because the application still depends on a static secret that must be rotated and protected.
distractor_2: Put the API key in the system prompt so the model can include it on requests - wrong because prompts are not a secure credential store and model output cannot authenticate API calls.
distractor_3: Create a shared access signature for the Azure OpenAI endpoint - wrong because Azure OpenAI does not use storage-style SAS tokens for request authentication.
not_a_variant_of: Prior q01 covers a Foundry project connection using managed identity for Azure AI Search; this row covers keyless application authentication to Azure OpenAI model deployments.

concept: Azure OpenAI function calling safeguards for side-effecting tools
scenario: An order assistant exposes get_order_status and create_return_label functions, and logs show that return labels are sometimes created during status-only conversations.
decisive_constraint: The application must allow harmless status lookups while blocking side-effecting return-label creation unless the user explicitly requests a return.
target_misconception: Candidates assume clearer prompt wording alone can safely control tool execution even when tool calls can trigger irreversible business actions.
correct_answer: Use tool choice or equivalent application routing to limit the functions available on status-only turns.
correct_answer_2: Validate the requested user intent in application code before executing any side-effecting function call.
distractor_1: Add more few-shot examples showing the preferred tool order - wrong because examples can influence the model but do not enforce a hard boundary before the function runs.
distractor_2: Increase the model temperature to make the assistant less repetitive - wrong because higher temperature makes tool selection less deterministic and does not protect side-effecting operations.
not_a_variant_of: Prior q03 covers submitting custom function outputs in the required-action loop; this row covers preventing unauthorized side effects before a function executes.

concept: Azure AI Search semantic ranker with extractive captions for RAG passages
scenario: A support-search API already blends vector and BM25 retrieval, but reviewers need short extractive captions from the highest-ranked policy passages returned to the agent.
decisive_constraint: The ranking step must apply language understanding over searchable text fields after the initial retrieval set is produced.
target_misconception: Candidates confuse vector similarity or indexer scheduling with semantic reranking features that generate captions and answers.
correct_answer: Enable semantic ranker with a semantic configuration and request extractive captions or answers in the search query.
distractor_1: Increase the HNSW efSearch value on the vector field - wrong because approximate vector search tuning can affect vector recall but does not produce semantic captions.
distractor_2: Schedule the indexer to run every five minutes - wrong because indexer frequency affects freshness, not semantic reranking or extractive caption generation.
distractor_3: Add an OCR skillset to the indexer - wrong because OCR extracts text from images and does not rerank already searchable text passages.
not_a_variant_of: Existing exams cover hybrid search and indexer freshness; this row focuses on semantic ranker outputs after retrieval.

concept: Azure AI Vision Read OCR for extracting text from standalone images
scenario: A field-service agent receives photos of equipment labels and must extract serial numbers from each image before sending the text to a troubleshooting prompt.
decisive_constraint: The input is a standalone image label rather than a multi-page business form with key-value pairs.
target_misconception: Candidates choose broad image captioning or form-recognition models when the requirement is precise OCR text extraction from an image.
correct_answer: Use Azure AI Vision Read OCR to extract printed or handwritten text from the equipment-label image.
distractor_1: Use the prebuilt invoice model in Azure AI Document Intelligence - wrong because invoice extraction targets invoice fields, not arbitrary equipment-label OCR.
distractor_2: Use image caption generation from Azure AI Vision Image Analysis - wrong because captions summarize image content instead of returning exact serial-number text.
distractor_3: Use Azure AI Speech batch transcription - wrong because transcription processes audio recordings, not text embedded in an image.
not_a_variant_of: Prior q05 covers captions and object regions, and prior q09 covers scanned invoice extraction; this row isolates OCR for standalone image text.

concept: Azure AI Language custom named entity recognition for domain-specific fields
scenario: A legal intake pipeline must extract renewal_notice_date and liability_cap from thousands of contract paragraphs for downstream review.
decisive_constraint: The required entities are domain-specific fields that built-in entity categories do not recognize, and the team can label representative contract examples.
target_misconception: Candidates confuse built-in NER, key phrase extraction, and sentiment analysis with trainable custom entity extraction.
correct_answer: Create and train a custom named entity recognition project in Azure AI Language using labeled contract examples.
distractor_1: Use prebuilt named entity recognition only - wrong because built-in categories do not include the custom contract fields required by the pipeline.
distractor_2: Use key phrase extraction - wrong because key phrases identify important terms but do not return typed entities such as renewal_notice_date.
distractor_3: Use sentiment analysis with opinion mining - wrong because sentiment features detect attitudes and opinions, not structured contract entities.
not_a_variant_of: Prior q15 covers PII detection and prior q26 covers sentiment with opinion mining; this row covers custom NER for domain-specific text fields.

concept: Azure AI Document Intelligence custom classifier for mixed document packets
scenario: An insurance upload contains driver licenses, repair estimates, and medical bills in one PDF, and each page must be routed to the correct extraction model.
decisive_constraint: The document type can change by page, so the workflow must identify document classes and page ranges before field extraction.
target_misconception: Candidates try to run one extraction model across a mixed packet instead of classifying documents before routing pages.
correct_answer: Train a Document Intelligence custom classification model with labeled examples for each document type.
correct_answer_2: Use the classifier output to split or route page ranges to the appropriate extraction model.
distractor_1: Run the prebuilt invoice model over the entire packet - wrong because invoice extraction will not correctly classify driver licenses or medical bills.
distractor_2: Send the PDF directly to Azure AI Search semantic ranker - wrong because semantic ranking orders indexed text results and does not classify page-level document types for extraction.
not_a_variant_of: Prior q17 covers custom neural extraction for one form family; this row covers classifying mixed document packets before extraction.

concept: Azure AI Foundry Agent Model Context Protocol tool integration
scenario: A build-support agent must query approved operations from a remote Model Context Protocol server that exposes build status and artifact lookup tools.
decisive_constraint: Tool discovery should come from the MCP server, while execution remains brokered through the Foundry Agent tool integration.
target_misconception: Candidates treat MCP endpoints as plain URLs to paste into prompts instead of tool providers whose operations are exposed through the agent tool list.
correct_answer: Configure the MCP server as an Azure AI Foundry Agent tool so the agent can discover and call its approved operations.
distractor_1: Paste the MCP server URL into the system prompt - wrong because a prompt string does not register callable tools or provide structured invocation metadata.
distractor_2: Embed yesterday's build logs in a vector index - wrong because retrieval over static logs cannot call live MCP operations for current build status.
distractor_3: Let the model emit arbitrary HTTP requests in free-form text - wrong because free-form text is not a brokered MCP tool call and bypasses approved operation schemas.
not_a_variant_of: Existing exams cover generic function schemas and prior q23 covers an OpenAPI tool; this row covers MCP server integration specifically.

concept: Azure OpenAI prompt caching for stable repeated prompt prefixes
scenario: A high-volume assistant sends the same 6000-token policy preamble followed by a short user question on every request, and repeated input tokens dominate latency and cost.
decisive_constraint: The team cannot remove the policy text yet, but the reusable prefix can remain identical across requests.
target_misconception: Candidates confuse prompt caching with context-window expansion or assume any repeated content is cached even when the prefix changes.
correct_answer: Keep the reusable policy instructions and context at the beginning of the prompt in a stable order so eligible requests can benefit from prompt caching.
distractor_1: Randomize policy-section order to improve coverage - wrong because changing the shared prefix prevents cache hits for otherwise repeated prompt content.
distractor_2: Increase max output tokens on each request - wrong because output-token allowance does not reduce repeated input-token processing cost.
distractor_3: Move the policy text to the end after the user question - wrong because prompt caching is based on matching repeated prefixes, not arbitrary repeated suffixes.
not_a_variant_of: Existing questions mention prompt caching only as a distractor for context overflow; this row tests cache-hit design for repeated prompt prefixes.

concept: Azure AI Foundry Agent grounding with Bing Search for current public web content
scenario: A travel-planning agent must answer questions about current public visa advisories that change daily and are not stored in the organization's private document index.
decisive_constraint: The agent needs fresh public web grounding rather than retrieval from a private file set or model retraining.
target_misconception: Candidates overuse private file search or fine-tuning when the requirement is fresh public-web grounding at answer time.
correct_answer: Add Grounding with Bing Search as an Azure AI Foundry Agent tool for current public web information.
distractor_1: Use the Agent File Search tool with an old PDF snapshot - wrong because a static private file index will not stay current with daily public advisories.
distractor_2: Fine-tune the model on last month's advisory pages - wrong because fine-tuning changes behavior but does not provide fresh public web facts at query time.
distractor_3: Put a reminder in the system prompt to be current - wrong because prompt instructions do not give the model access to newly updated web content.
not_a_variant_of: Prior q13 covers File Search over indexed private policy documents; this row covers fresh public web grounding through Bing Search.

concept: Azure AI Translator text translation before English-only downstream analysis
scenario: A product-feedback agent receives Spanish, French, and English comments, but its downstream summarization prompt and taxonomy are maintained only in English.
decisive_constraint: The pipeline must preserve the comment meaning while normalizing non-English feedback before the English-only analysis step.
target_misconception: Candidates confuse language detection or sentiment scoring with the translation step required before English-only downstream prompting.
correct_answer: Use Azure AI Translator text translation to translate non-English feedback into English before running the downstream analysis prompt.
distractor_1: Run language detection and stop there - wrong because detecting the source language does not convert the comment into English for the downstream taxonomy.
distractor_2: Run sentiment analysis on the original text only - wrong because sentiment scores do not produce translated text for English-only summarization.
distractor_3: Use speech-to-text transcription - wrong because the input is already written text, not audio that needs transcription.
not_a_variant_of: Prior q07 covers real-time speech translation from audio; this row covers text translation as preprocessing for English-only analysis.

concept: Azure OpenAI Batch API for asynchronous non-latency-sensitive prompt processing
scenario: An AI engineer must summarize 80,000 archived chat transcripts overnight with Azure OpenAI, and no user is waiting on an interactive response.
decisive_constraint: The workload can complete asynchronously within the batch completion window, but each output must remain traceable to the input transcript ID.
target_misconception: Candidates confuse offline batch inference with online chat completions capacity, fine-tuning, or a deployed prompt flow endpoint.
correct_answer: Submit the transcript requests as a JSONL input file to the Azure OpenAI Batch API and retrieve the completed output file.
distractor_1: Move the chat deployment to provisioned throughput - wrong because provisioned throughput improves reserved online capacity but does not create an asynchronous batch job with input-to-output file mapping.
distractor_2: Fine-tune the model on the transcript archive - wrong because fine-tuning changes model behavior and does not execute the one-time summarization workload.
distractor_3: Deploy the prompt as a real-time prompt flow endpoint - wrong because an endpoint is still an online serving path and does not match an overnight batch processing requirement.
not_a_variant_of: No prior row covers Azure OpenAI Batch API; q02 covers deployment capacity for predictable live traffic and q38 covers prompt caching.

concept: Azure AI Search integrated vectorization during indexer-based ingestion
scenario: A developer is indexing PDF files from Azure Blob Storage into Azure AI Search and wants chunking plus embedding generation to happen inside the indexing pipeline.
decisive_constraint: The team must avoid a separate embedding batch job while still populating a vector field compatible with the search index profile.
target_misconception: Candidates know vector search but miss that integrated vectorization requires both indexer enrichment and vector index configuration.
correct_answer: Configure Azure AI Search integrated vectorization with an indexer and skillset that chunks content and calls an embedding model during ingestion.
correct_answer_2: Define the vector field, vector search profile, and embedding dimensions in the index so generated vectors can be stored and searched.
distractor_1: Use the semantic ranker by itself - wrong because semantic ranking reranks textual results and does not generate or store embeddings.
distractor_2: Push precomputed vectors from an external script only - wrong because that bypasses the required indexer-based integrated vectorization pipeline.
not_a_variant_of: Existing concepts cover hybrid search, semantic ranking, push indexes, and chunking; this row tests indexer-based integrated vectorization.

concept: GPT-4o vision input for multimodal chart and screenshot reasoning
scenario: A support copilot must answer questions about screenshots that contain UI state, icons, and a small performance chart rather than only extracting visible text.
decisive_constraint: The answer depends on reasoning over the screenshot layout and chart trend, not just OCR text.
target_misconception: Candidates over-apply OCR, object detection, or document extraction when the scenario needs multimodal model reasoning over an image.
correct_answer: Send the screenshot as image input to a vision-capable GPT-4o model deployment and ask the model to reason over the visual content.
distractor_1: Use Azure AI Vision Read OCR only - wrong because OCR extracts text but does not reason over chart trends and UI relationships.
distractor_2: Use Azure AI Vision object detection only - wrong because object regions do not provide the natural-language reasoning needed for the chart and UI state.
distractor_3: Use Azure AI Document Intelligence invoice extraction - wrong because invoice field extraction is specialized for structured documents, not arbitrary screenshots.
not_a_variant_of: q05 covers Azure AI Vision captions and object regions, and q34 covers Read OCR; this row covers GPT-4o vision reasoning.

concept: Semantic Kernel plugins exposing native functions to an agent
scenario: A C# travel assistant built with Semantic Kernel must call existing pricing and reservation methods with strongly typed arguments during an itinerary conversation.
decisive_constraint: The existing methods should be discoverable as callable functions without rewriting them as a separate REST API.
target_misconception: Candidates confuse Semantic Kernel plugins with prompt-only instructions, search indexes, or multi-agent handoffs.
correct_answer: Wrap the native methods as Semantic Kernel plugin functions and add the plugin to the kernel used by the agent.
distractor_1: List the method names in the system prompt - wrong because prompt text does not expose executable typed functions to the Semantic Kernel runtime.
distractor_2: Create an Azure AI Search index over the method documentation - wrong because search can retrieve descriptions but cannot invoke the pricing and reservation methods.
distractor_3: Create a connected agent for each method - wrong because connected agents route work to other agents and are unnecessary for exposing local native functions.
not_a_variant_of: q23 covers Azure AI Foundry OpenAPI tools and q24 covers connected agents; this row covers Semantic Kernel native plugins.

concept: Azure AI Foundry model catalog filtering by task and modality before deployment
scenario: A solution architect must choose a deployable model for an app that analyzes both images and text, and the review board wants the provider, modality, and license checked before deployment.
decisive_constraint: The selection must happen before creating a deployment and must compare model capabilities across providers in Azure AI Foundry.
target_misconception: Candidates confuse the model catalog selection step with quota management, search indexing, or safety screening.
correct_answer: Use the Azure AI Foundry model catalog to filter and compare models by task, modality, provider, and deployment availability.
distractor_1: Open the quota management page first - wrong because quota pages show capacity limits and do not compare model modality or provider suitability.
distractor_2: Create an Azure AI Search index first - wrong because search indexes support retrieval and do not select the multimodal model to deploy.
distractor_3: Run Azure AI Content Safety before model selection - wrong because safety screening evaluates content risk and does not compare deployable model capabilities.
not_a_variant_of: Existing concepts mention managed workspaces and model deployment capacity; no prior row tests model catalog filtering by modality and provider.

concept: Azure OpenAI parallel function calling for independent read-only tools
scenario: A customer service agent must check shipment ETA and warranty status in the same turn, and both lookups are read-only calls to independent backend systems.
decisive_constraint: The latency target is two seconds, and neither lookup depends on the result of the other.
target_misconception: Candidates serialize independent tool calls or merge unrelated tools instead of using parallel function calling safely.
correct_answer: Allow the model to issue parallel function calls for the ETA and warranty lookup functions when the selected model supports it.
correct_answer_2: Keep the functions independently executable and read-only so parallel execution cannot create ordering-dependent side effects.
distractor_1: Force a single serial planning loop for every tool call - wrong because serialization adds latency when the two lookups have no dependency.
distractor_2: Merge shipment and warranty into one broad tool with loosely typed arguments - wrong because that weakens schema boundaries and does not use parallel independent calls.
not_a_variant_of: q32 covers safeguards for side-effecting tools; this row covers parallel execution of independent read-only function calls.

concept: Azure AI Video Indexer extraction of transcripts, scenes, and insights for searchable video archives
scenario: A training portal must make two-hour product videos searchable by spoken transcript, detected topics, scene changes, and key visual moments.
decisive_constraint: The ingestion step must produce time-coded video insights that can be indexed for later discovery.
target_misconception: Candidates pick a single-modality service and miss that the requirement is video insight extraction across speech and visuals.
correct_answer: Use Azure AI Video Indexer to extract transcripts, scenes, topics, and time-coded insights from each video.
distractor_1: Use Azure AI Document Intelligence layout extraction - wrong because layout extraction targets documents and does not analyze video timelines.
distractor_2: Use Speech batch transcription only - wrong because transcription captures spoken words but not scenes, topics, and visual moments in the video.
distractor_3: Use Azure AI Vision Read OCR on sampled frames only - wrong because frame OCR misses spoken transcript and video-level insights.
not_a_variant_of: Prior rows cover still-image analysis, OCR, speech translation, and conversation transcription; this row covers video indexing.

concept: Azure OpenAI content filtering severity thresholds for deployed chat applications
scenario: A public chat app using Azure OpenAI must block high-severity violent and self-harm content while allowing benign low-risk discussion about safety procedures.
decisive_constraint: The policy must be enforced by the Azure OpenAI content filtering system on model inputs and outputs, not only by prompt wording.
target_misconception: Candidates confuse configurable content filters with Prompt Shields, model temperature, or application copy changes.
correct_answer: Configure the Azure OpenAI content filter policy and severity thresholds for the deployment's input and output filtering behavior.
distractor_1: Lower the model temperature - wrong because temperature changes sampling randomness and does not enforce harm-category severity thresholds.
distractor_2: Add a system prompt asking users to avoid unsafe content - wrong because prompt wording is not an enforcement mechanism for Azure OpenAI filtering.
distractor_3: Enable Prompt Shields only - wrong because Prompt Shields target jailbreak and indirect prompt injection attacks, not general harm-category severity filtering.
not_a_variant_of: q14 covers Prompt Shields for indirect prompt injection and q25 covers image harm classification; this row covers Azure OpenAI text content filter thresholds.

concept: Azure AI Language key phrase extraction for salient phrases in support comments
scenario: An operations dashboard receives thousands of short support comments each day and must display the most important phrases without training a custom model.
decisive_constraint: The output should be extracted phrases from each comment, not a sentiment score or custom entity type.
target_misconception: Candidates confuse key phrase extraction with sentiment analysis, custom NER, or generative summarization.
correct_answer: Use Azure AI Language key phrase extraction on the support comments.
distractor_1: Use opinion mining sentiment analysis - wrong because opinion mining returns sentiment about targets rather than a list of salient phrases.
distractor_2: Train a custom named entity recognition model - wrong because custom NER is for labeled entity categories and is unnecessary when no custom type is required.
distractor_3: Ask a chat model to write a paragraph summary for each comment - wrong because the requirement is phrase extraction at dashboard scale, not generated summaries.
not_a_variant_of: q26 covers sentiment with opinion mining and q35 covers custom NER; this row covers built-in key phrase extraction.

concept: Azure AI Foundry red teaming for adversarial safety testing before release
scenario: Before launching a tool-using benefits assistant, a security lead wants adversarial tests for jailbreak attempts, unsafe content, and policy bypasses with a report for remediation.
decisive_constraint: The test must actively probe the application for safety failures before production release rather than only monitor live traffic afterward.
target_misconception: Candidates treat red teaming as ordinary content filtering, load testing, or manual prompt review.
correct_answer: Run Azure AI Foundry red teaming or safety evaluation workflows to generate adversarial attacks and review the resulting risk findings.
distractor_1: Rely only on the runtime content filter - wrong because filtering blocks known content categories but does not actively generate adversarial test cases before release.
distractor_2: Run an HTTP load test against the chat endpoint - wrong because load testing measures throughput and latency, not jailbreak or unsafe-content resilience.
distractor_3: Ask reviewers to read the system prompt manually - wrong because prompt review does not exercise the deployed application against adversarial conversations.
not_a_variant_of: q29 covers production quality and safety monitoring; this row covers pre-release adversarial red teaming.
